ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general shrimp/prawn food product class; no specific grilled tiger prawn entry exists. FOODON_03301116 (shrimp food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers shrimps and prawns as a category; no grilled tiger prawn-specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144002 covers prawn as a food substance; grilled preparation variant not separately coded.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of grilled tiger prawns consists of approximately 4 to 6 whole tiger prawns, weighing around 250 g in total including shells, equivalent to roughly 150–170 g edible flesh.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 15152); cross-referenced with USDA Foundation Foods — Shrimp, raw (FDC ID 175180) adjusted for grilling
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact grilled tiger prawn entry exists in USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp (NDB 15152) and USDA Foundation Foods raw shrimp (FDC 175180). Grilling causes minor moisture loss (~10–15%) and slight concentration of protein and minerals relative to boiled values. Olive oil and herb marinade contributes a modest fat increment (~1–2 g/100g). Values represent a best-estimate composite for grilled tiger prawns as served in a Turkish restaurant context.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous protein-dominant seafood entries
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for grilled tiger prawns. Tiger prawns are a high-protein, very low-carbohydrate food (0.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g). By analogy with other cooked crustaceans and protein-dominant seafood in the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently yield GI values in the range of 5–10 (low category), a GI of 5 is assigned. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (5 × 1.25 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.06, rounded to 0.4 accounting for the full serving carbohydrate content of ~8 g (including marinade ingredients). GL is effectively negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g); restaurant preparations may add additional salt or sauces
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful that a 250 g restaurant serving of grilled tiger prawns may contribute approximately 775 mg sodium, representing roughly one-third of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by major cardiovascular guidelines; requesting preparation without added salt or sauces can substantially reduce sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (0.5 g/100 g) and high protein (22.8 g/100 g) with negligible glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled tiger prawns are an excellent protein source with a negligible glycemic load (GL < 1 per serving), making them a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes who are managing postprandial blood glucose; the lean protein content also supports satiety without contributing to hyperglycaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol content (~189 mg/100 g) offset by low saturated fat and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While tiger prawns are relatively high in dietary cholesterol (~473 mg per 250 g serving), current evidence suggests dietary cholesterol from seafood has a modest effect on LDL in most individuals; the low saturated fat content (0.5 g/100 g) and presence of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids are cardioprotective, and moderate consumption is generally considered acceptable within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled tiger prawns (Penaeus monodon) are a premium seafood dish featuring large whole prawns marinated in olive oil, garlic, lemon juice, salt, pepper, and Mediterranean herbs such as parsley and oregano, then cooked over a high-heat grill until the shells are lightly charred and the flesh is tender and juicy. In Turkish cuisine, this dish — known as 'Izgara Karides' — is a classic meze or main course served in coastal restaurants and is particularly popular in Istanbul and along the Aegean and Mediterranean coasts. The prawns are typically served whole with lemon wedges and fresh herbs, sometimes accompanied by rice pilaf or grilled vegetables. The texture is firm yet succulent, with a naturally sweet, briny flavour enhanced by the smokiness of the grill and the brightness of lemon. Nutritionally, grilled tiger prawns are an excellent source of lean protein (~22.8 g/100 g), selenium, phosphorus, vitamin B12, and iodine, while being very low in carbohydrates (0.5 g/100 g) and saturated fat (0.5 g/100 g). The glycemic index is extremely low (estimated GI ~5), making this dish highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or following low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The cholesterol content is notable (~189 mg/100 g), but current dietary guidelines do not restrict seafood cholesterol for most healthy individuals. Sodium content from restaurant preparation warrants attention for those managing hypertension. In Singapore, this dish is served at Turkish and Lebanese restaurants such as Sultan Palace at Boat Quay, where it is priced at approximately SGD 38.90 per portion.
common_user_queries: How many calories in grilled tiger prawns?
Is izgara karides high in cholesterol?
What is the protein content of grilled tiger prawns?
Are grilled prawns good for weight loss?
What nutrients are in tiger prawns?
How much sodium in grilled prawns?

confidence_reason: No exact grilled tiger prawn entry was found in any primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp data (SR Legacy NDB 15152 and Foundation Foods FDC 175180), which represents the highest-quality available approved source for this food type. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database protein-dominant seafood category. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked/grilled shrimp data; no exact grilled tiger prawn entry exists in any approved primary regional source. 2. Fat values include a modest increment (~1–2 g/100 g) to account for olive oil marinade used in Turkish-style preparation. 3. Sodium value (310 mg/100 g) reflects cooked shrimp with light seasoning; restaurant preparations may be higher. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked shrimp; naturally zero for this seafood product with no industrial hydrogenation. 5. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; naturally zero for plain grilled crustacean flesh. 6. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; naturally zero as this is a pure animal protein food with no plant fibre. 7. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy shrimp entry used as the primary source. 8. GI of 5 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database protein-dominant seafood category; no direct GI measurement for grilled tiger prawns was found in the peer-reviewed literature. 9. Cholesterol content (~189 mg/100 g) is notable; individuals with familial hypercholesterolaemia should consult a dietitian. 10. This dish is served at Sultan Palace, Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey listing, 2 restaurant entries). 11. Multilingual names provided for Turkish cuisine served in Singapore context; Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names are included to support Singapore multilingual retrieval. Turkish romanized aliases (Izgara Karides, Izgara Karides) are included for Turkish cuisine authenticity.

improvement_actions_taken: Removed menu number prefix '57.' from food_name to produce canonical English name.
Added multilingual local names for Singapore context (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) and Turkish romanized aliases.
Adapted nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked shrimp with documented fat increment for olive oil marinade.
Assigned GI of 5 as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database protein-dominant seafood category with full documentation.
Documented all zero values (trans_fat_g, sugar_g, dietary_fibre_g) with 'confirmed zero' and 'naturally zero' justifications in notes.
Updated documentation for vitamin_d_mcg = -1 to explicitly state 'not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy shrimp entry used as the primary source' for G3 compliance.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent — all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in aliases.
Used only allowed ontology match_type values: exact, closest_parent, none.
Set food_group to 'Fish and seafood' as this is a pure seafood dish, overriding the input 'Mixed' classification.
